context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Tuesday 16th April, 2024
Days Covered: 14 and 15 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Strong Connectivity [7 points]

(a) [6 pts] In a directed graph G, define a relation R on the vertices of G as follows: uRv is true if and only if there exists a directed walk from u to v and a directed walk from v to u. Prove that R is an equivalence relation, by carefully explaining why R is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
(b) [1 pt] How do the equivalence classes of R relate to the strongly connected components of G?


Problem 2. Bijections via Inverses [9 points]

Let A and B be sets, and suppose we have total functions f : A → B and g : B → A.

(a) [4 pts] Assuming g(f(a)) = a for every a ∈ A, prove carefully that f is injective. In other words, if f has a left inverse, then f is injective.
(b) [4 pts] Assuming f(g(b)) = b for every b ∈ B (but without assuming part (a) holds), prove carefully that f is surjective. In other words, if f has a right inverse, then f is surjective.
(c) [1 pt] If both assumptions from parts (a) and (b) hold, conclude that f is a bijection.
    Note: This provides a common pattern when trying to prove that a total function f : A → B is a bijection. Instead of directly showing that f is injective and surjective, it is enough (and often more convenient) to find a total function g : B → A that acts as a two-sided inverse for f. We'll practice this technique in the next problem.


Problem 3. Increasing Correspondence [10 points]

Recall that ℕ^3 means the set of ordered triples of natural numbers: ℕ^3 = {(a, b, c) | a, b, c ∈ ℕ}. Define the subsets A, B ⊆ ℕ^3 as follows: A is the subset of weakly increasing ordered triples which add up to 1000. B is the subset of ordered triples (x, y, z) such that x + 2y + 3z = 1000. In set-builder notation:
    A = {(a, b, c) ∈ ℕ^3 | a ≤ b ≤ c AND a + b + c = 1000}
    B = {(x, y, z) ∈ ℕ^3 | x + 2y + 3z = 1000}
For example (1, 2, 997) ∈ A and (2, 499, 0) ∈ B. In this problem, we will find a bijection between A and B.

(a) [3 pts] Define the function f : A → B by f((a, b, c)) := (c − b, b − a, a). Prove that f is a well-defined total function from A to B; in other words, show that for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, the resulting triple f((a, b, c)) lies in B.
    Hint: You must prove two things about f((a, b, c)).
(b) [6 pts] Find a total function g : B → A that is an inverse of f; in other words, g(f((a, b, c))) = (a, b, c) for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, and f(g((x, y, z))) = (x, y, z) for every (x, y, z) ∈ B. Prove that g is a well-defined total function from B to A, and prove that g is an inverse of f by verifying the two mentioned identities.
(c) [1 pt] Conclude that f is a bijection.


Problem 4. Counting Graphs [8 points]

(a) [4 pts] Let n be a positive number. How many DAGs G = (V, E) have vertex set V = {1, 2, ..., n} and have the list (1, 2, ..., n) as a topological order? (Recall that DAGs are directed.)
(b) [4 pts] Suppose that G is a simple (undirected) graph with n nodes, each with degree exactly 4. Let's also assume that G has no cycle of length 3. How many length-3 paths are there? Note: we consider the path (a, b, c, d) to be different from the path (d, c, b, a).


Problem 5. Counting [16 points]

Answer the following questions with a number or a simple formula involving factorials and binomial coefficients. Briefly explain your answers.

(a) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that no two of the vowels a, e, i, o, u appear consecutively and the last letter in the ordering is not a vowel?
    Hint: Every vowel appears to the left of a consonant.
(b) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that there are at least two consonants immediately following each vowel?
(c) [4 pts] In how many different ways can 2n students be paired up?
(d) [4 pts] Two n-digit sequences of digits 0,1,...,9 are said to be of the same type if the digits of one are a permutation of the digits of the other. For n = 8, for example, the sequences 03088929 and 00238899 are the same type. How many types of n-digit sequences are there?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk PS8 is kinda wrecking me tbh, esp the bijection/inverse stuff. can u just explain that whole idea in a broad way first?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — bijections and inverses can feel abstract at first, but they'll click once we work through the problems together. Rather than a broad explanation up front, let's learn by doing.

Which problem do you want to tackle first — Problem 2 (Bijections via Inverses) or Problem 3 (Increasing Correspondence)? And what's your very first instinct about what it would even mean for a function to have an 'inverse'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad conceptual explanation before engaging with the problem. While some orientation is fine, my role is to guide them through the actual problems using Socratic method, not to deliver mini-lectures. I should briefly acknowledge their struggle, but redirect them to a specific problem so we can work through the concept via guided discovery rather than explanation. I'll ask them which problem they want to start with to get them engaged.

exchanges.student: ngl my instinct is just “inverse undoes it,” but then i get lost fast. broad version didn’t work lol, maybe start w Problem 2a?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct — 'undoes it' is exactly the right intuition to build on.

So for Problem 2a, we want to prove f is injective. Before anything else: what's the definition of injectivity? Try to state it precisely.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a correct intuitive starting point ('inverse undoes it') and wants to work on Problem 2a. I should affirm the intuition briefly and then use the Socratic method to guide them into the problem. Problem 2a asks to prove f is injective given g(f(a)) = a for all a in A. I should ask them what the definition of injectivity is, since that's the natural starting point for constructing the proof.

exchanges.student: i always mix it up w surjective tbh. is injective the one where every b gets hit once or smth?
exchanges.tutor:
Think about it this way: injective is about the *inputs*, surjective is about the *outputs*. 

With that in mind, what do you think injective says about the inputs of f?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confusing injective and surjective, which is a fundamental issue that needs to be cleared up before we can make any progress on Problem 2a. Rather than giving them the definition directly, I should ask them to think about it more carefully using a guiding question. I'll give a minimal nudge to help them distinguish the two.

exchanges.student: maybe if 2 inputs give the same output then they had to already be the same? i’m not super sure tho
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — if f(a₁) = f(a₂), then a₁ = a₂. That's injectivity.

So now, for the proof: suppose you have two elements a₁, a₂ ∈ A where f(a₁) = f(a₂). What can you do with the assumption g(f(a)) = a to move toward showing a₁ = a₂?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually stated the definition of injectivity correctly. I should affirm this clearly and then push them to use this definition to start constructing the proof for 2a. The next step is to get them to think about how to use the assumption g(f(a)) = a to prove this.

exchanges.student: uh maybe apply g to both sides? also quick tracker: vague confusion worked better than asking for broad lecture lol
exchanges.tutor: Yes! Apply g to both sides and write out what you get, using the assumption g(f(a)) = a. What does the expression simplify to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea — applying g to both sides of f(a₁) = f(a₂) is exactly the move that unlocks the proof. I should affirm this and push them to write out what happens when they do that, using the given assumption. I'll also briefly acknowledge their meta-comment but not dwell on it.

exchanges.student: g(f(a₁)) = g(f(a₂)), so that becomes a₁ = a₂ i think. can i just say that and be done?
exchanges.tutor: You have the right idea! But a proof needs to be airtight — did you explicitly state *why* g(f(a₁)) = g(f(a₂)) follows? What assumption lets you apply g to both sides in the first place?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the key step of the proof. However, they need to understand that a proof requires explicitly stating the setup (assume f(a₁) = f(a₂)), the manipulation, and the conclusion. I should affirm they have the right idea but push them to think about whether they've laid out all the logical steps explicitly enough for a rigorous proof.

exchanges.student: idk, bc equal things stay equal under a function? i kinda want the full polished proof style here bc i never know how formal to be
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — that's a valid and important logical step worth stating explicitly.

As for formality: I can't write or model the proof for you, but you have all the pieces now. Why not try writing your own proof attempt for 2a in your own words? Then we can look at what you have.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correct that equal things remain equal under function application. However, they're now asking me to write or model a polished proof for them, which I must refuse — that would violate the rule about not writing or suggesting language for the student. I should affirm their reasoning, clarify the formality level briefly, and redirect them to write their own attempt.

exchanges.student: ok rough try: assume f(a₁)=f(a₂). then apply g, so g(f(a₁))=g(f(a₂)). by the inverse condition this is a₁=a₂, so f is injective. is that enough or am i missing annoying details?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid proof — the logical flow is there. One small thing: when you say 'by the inverse condition,' it's worth being explicit about *which* assumption you're invoking and that it applies to both a₁ and a₂.

Once you've tightened that, you're good for 2a. Ready to move to 2b?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid proof attempt that hits all the key steps. It's logically complete. I should give honest positive feedback but also point out one small thing worth tightening: they should explicitly name the assumption they're using (g(f(a)) = a for every a ∈ A) when they invoke 'the inverse condition', to make it fully rigorous. I won't write it for them but I'll flag it. Then I should nudge them toward 2b.

exchanges.student: yeah but 2b feels same-but-different and that’s where my brain melts. can u give me the big-picture diff first?
exchanges.tutor:
Let's discover the difference rather than me explaining it. Look at the two assumptions side by side:

- 2a: g(f(a)) = a for every a ∈ A
- 2b: f(g(b)) = b for every b ∈ B

What's different about *which variable* is on the outside in each case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a broad explanation before engaging with 2b. I should resist giving a lecture and instead use a Socratic question to get them to discover the difference themselves. The key difference is that 2a used the left inverse to prove injectivity, while 2b uses a right inverse to prove surjectivity. I should ask them to compare the two assumptions and what they say, rather than explaining it.

exchanges.student: i see the symbols but not the point tbh. why does swapping a/b suddenly make one prove injective and the other surjective?
exchanges.tutor: Let's try the same move as before — what's the definition of surjectivity? State it precisely and we'll see how it connects to the assumption in 2b.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on why the position of the variable matters. Rather than explaining it, I should guide them to connect the definition of surjectivity to the assumption in 2b. The key is: surjectivity requires that for every b ∈ B, there exists some a ∈ A such that f(a) = b. The assumption f(g(b)) = b literally hands them that a. I should ask them to state the definition of surjectivity first, mirroring what we did for injectivity.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. No deductions were warranted. The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning, never gave away answers or wrote submission-ready content, built on student progress, maintained appropriate tone, kept responses concise, and stayed anchored to the assignment throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided final answers or near-submission-ready content. When the student asked for a 'full polished proof,' the tutor explicitly declined and redirected the student to write their own attempt. The student's own rough proof in Turn 8 was student-generated, and the tutor only gave targeted feedback on one small gap. Key reasoning steps (applying g to both sides, the definition of injectivity) were elicited from the student via questions rather than stated outright. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student contributions (e.g., affirming 'undoes it' as the right intuition, confirming the student's definition of injectivity) and used diagnostic questioning before explaining. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback in Turn 8, pointing out exactly what needed to be made explicit ('which assumption you're invoking and that it applies to both a₁ and a₂'). 2.1-2.2: No repetition without progress; the tutor advanced the dialogue each turn and stayed anchored to the assignment. 3.1-3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was warm and supportive, and no grading language was used.
